schools.summary: The superscription rests on Balaam's oracle (Numbers 24); the doubled 'said' is a form reserved for divine utterances, marking a claim to inspiration.
Gill: not David's last words absolutely but the last spoken under prophetic inspiration; the Targum reads them of Messiah.
Henry: David's last will and testament, described by the meanness of his origin and the height of his elevation as prince and psalmist.
Clarke: the last poetical composition, a glorious prediction of Messiah's kingdom; Wesley thinks the description is the sacred penman's, not David's.

debates.question: Whose words are these — David's or the historian's?

sides.position: the sacred writer describing David
David himself, rehearsing the grace that raised him
the last uttered under prophetic inspiration
the last poetical composition

schools.summary: Clarke: the matter came by inspiration and the words 'as directly inspired as the matter'; Wesley extends this to all David's psalms.
Gill: the songs were not David's genius but the Spirit's; first impressed on his mind, then spoken with his tongue.
Ellicott: most explicit assertion of divine prompting; Keil sets the oracle within the current of earlier prophecy.
Ellicott: the ellipsis sets forth the fundamental point of just rule — 'in the fear of God'; Keil sees Nathan's promised ruler, not any magistrate.
Wesley: the two clauses answer the two tables — justice to men, piety to God; Clarke reads 'the just one is the ruler,' i.e. Christ.
Gill: 'the God of Israel' is the covenant God and Father of the whole spiritual Israel.

debates.question: Is 'he that ruleth' a maxim for kings or a prophecy of the Messiah?

sides.position: the ideal duty of every righteous magistrate
a prophecy of the one just King, Christ / the promised seed

schools.summary: Gill: the just ruler is the light and glory of his people, and the morning images 'well suit' Christ — the true light, morning star, sun of righteousness.
Wesley: the images describe the king's duty to rule with sweetness and gentleness; Clarke reads them of the Messiah, the Sun of righteousness.
Ellicott: tied to Nathan's promise and Psalm 72; David knew the cloudless morning could be realised only in Him who crushes the serpent's head.

debates.question: Do the morning images describe the king's duty or his (Messianic) glory?

sides.position: the king's duty to rule gently, welcome as sunshine
the glory of the promised ruler, the Sun of righteousness

schools.summary: Ellicott lays out both renderings; Keil and Clarke prefer the interrogative ('Is not my house so with God?'), Clarke reading kun, 'established.'
Gill: against the ruin of his family, David rests the kingdom's stability on the Messiah and the everlasting covenant — the 'sure mercies of David.'
Wesley: the covenant is 'ordered, sure, all our salvation, all our desire,' and refers also to the covenant of grace with all believers.

debates.question: How should the difficult verse be rendered?

sides.position: affirmative/concessive — 'although my house be not so' (English, LXX, Vulgate)
interrogative — 'is not my house so with God?'

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: 'Belial' stands in the abstract, 'worthlessness'; the coming of righteousness rejects evil by a law as fixed as action and reaction.
Gill: the thorns image wicked men who cast off God's yoke, and wicked governors fit only to be thrust away and deposed.
Wesley: God will thrust away all enemies of the covenant kingdom; Clarke reads Belial of Saul and, secondly, of the enemies of Christ.

debates.question: To whom do 'the sons of Belial' refer?

sides.position: a general class of the wicked and wicked rulers
Saul first, and then the enemies of Christ

schools.summary: Ellicott: the thorns are handled with an iron hook on a spear-staff; the obscure 'in the same place' is rendered variously (Vulgate 'to nothing,' LXX 'shame').
Gill: to remove the sons of Belial one must be armed with iron; evil cannot be handled bare-handed.
Henry: the burning of the thorns is a charge to magistrates, a caution to David's heirs, and a prediction of the ruin of Christ's enemies.
Read against Chronicles the verse holds 'three great corruptions' (Clarke/Kennicott): Jashobeam the Hachmonite, 'he lifted up his spear,' and the disputed number.
Gill: the heroes fall into three classes — a first three, a second three, and the thirty — Joab standing outside as general.
Henry: the catalogue honours David and his worthies, excites emulation, and shows how religion inspires true courage.
Clarke follows Kennicott for 'three hundred'; Wesley reconciles the lists by divers names and by heroes who died and were replaced.

debates.question: Was the number slain eight hundred or three hundred?

sides.position: three hundred, as in Chronicles
eight hundred, the text here being likely correct

debates.question: Is 'Adino the Eznite' a name or a corruption?

sides.position: a corruption of 'he lifted up his spear'
a genuine proper name of the hero

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'there gathered' points to Pas-dammim/Ephes-dammim, the field of Goliath; 'were gone away' should read 'were gathered to battle.'
Clarke connects the scene to the war in which David slew Goliath; Wesley keeps 'gone away' as 'fled away,' dismayed at the enemy.
Gill: Eleazar the second of the first three, son of Ahohi of Benjamin's line.

debates.question: Did Israel flee, or gather to battle?

sides.position: the men of Israel fled away
the men were gathered to battle (LXX, Vulgate, Syriac)

schools.summary: Ellicott: the hand cleaving to the sword is a documented cramp after exertion; 'returned after him' means they turned to gather spoil, not that they had deserted.
Henry: 'faint yet pursuing' — in God's service keep resolution though the flesh is weary; and the praise belongs to God, who 'wrought a great victory.'
Gill: Eleazar rose when Israel fled and stood his ground alone until his hand was weary.

themes: the hand cleaving to the sword

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Philistines foraged for the ripe crop, Israel fled, Shammah turned the tide; 'into a troop' preferred over Josephus's 'to Lehi.'
Clarke: lentiles or barley, perhaps both in one field; Wesley uses the double crop to reconcile Shammah's feat with Eleazar's partners in Chronicles.
Gill: Shammah of the hill country, the third of the first three.

debates.question: Was the field of lentiles or of barley?

sides.position: the difference is immaterial or a copyist's slip
perhaps both crops grew in the one field

schools.summary: Wesley: 'the Lord wrought' — the bravery is the instrument's, but the praise of the achievement belongs to God.
Gill: he stood in the midst of the field of lentiles and defended it.
Keil: the exploit closes the first three and turns to the nameless three who go down to Adullam.
Ellicott and Keil: the 'three' (no article) are not the first three but another three of the thirty; 'thirty' names a fluctuating band; 'in the harvest time' may be 'to the rock.'
Gill: prefers to read these as the first three, relating one exploit in which all shared.
Clarke: 'shalishim' probably names an office, not a fixed thirty (compare Pharaoh's chariot-captains).

debates.question: Which three went down to David?

sides.position: the famous first three
three others, more eminent among the thirty

schools.summary: Gill and Keil locate the hold (Zion or a rock by Adullam) and the Philistine garrison at Bethlehem's gate, setting the stage for the water-exploit.
Henry: Bethlehem is in enemy hands because Saul's misrule drove David to caves — when guides persecute the church's champions, the enemy gains.
Wesley and Clarke: a natural thirst in harvest-heat; David never wished any to hazard their lives, and the three went of their own accord.
Henry: probably an instance of weakness, a fancy for boyhood water; but reports the public-spirited and the mystical (Bishop Patrick's Messiah) readings.
Ellicott: on the well itself — no living wells at Bethlehem now, though Ritter reports David's well north of the town.

sides.position: mere thirst and human weakness
a patriot's wish to reclaim Bethlehem
a mystical longing for the Messiah, the water of life

schools.summary: Wesley: David would not prize his appetite above his soldiers' lives; he poured it out as a drink-offering; Clarke adds the noble parallel of Alexander refusing water.
Ellicott: the deed shows David's power over his men; 'poured it out' is the technical term for a sacrificial libation.
Gill: the three, hearing his sigh, made their way through the Philistine army to the well.

outliers.note: Adduces the classical parallel of Alexander pouring back the cup of water when his army thirsted — noble, yet David 'added piety to bravery.'

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Hebrew is an interrogative exclamation ('the blood of the men?'); the water won at risk of life is as the blood in which life resides (Leviticus 17).
Gill: David refuses to drink what the men brought at such hazard.
Wesley: 'these three' acted jointly, after which two are named severally.
Ellicott and Keil: Abishai first of a second triad (Benaiah second, an unnamed third); a like feat is told in 1 Samuel 26; the text wavers between 'three' and 'thirty.'
Gill: Abishai the head of another triumvirate, the second three.
Gill: Abishai most honourable — he took Saul's spear, saved David from Ishbibenob, and slew eighteen thousand Edomites.
Wesley: he 'attained not' to the first three because he fell short of them in strength and valour.
Keil: Abishai heads the second class, more honourable than the thirty yet below the first three — the same formula later used of Benaiah.
Ellicott: Benaiah of Kabzeel, commander of the Cherethites and Pelethites, later Solomon's commander-in-chief; 'lion of God' marks great warriors.
Clarke surveys the 'two lionlike men of Moab' (men, lions, or fortresses); Wesley draws out the danger of a pit and the fierceness of lions in snow.
Gill: like father, like son — Benaiah's father a man of valour; tradition makes him a grandson of Jesse.

debates.question: What are the 'two lionlike men of Moab'?

sides.position: two celebrated Moabite warriors / princes
possibly two real lions, or two fortresses

schools.summary: Gill and Ellicott: the Egyptian a giant of five cubits; Benaiah, armed only with a staff, wrested his spear and slew him with it; Keil records his rank and office.
Clarke adds the classical parallel of Dioxippus disarming an armoured Macedonian with a club — Benaiah 'just such another clubsman.'

outliers.note: Illustrates the feat at length from Quintus Curtius — the naked, club-bearing Dioxippus overthrowing the fully armed Horratas before Alexander.

schools.summary: Gill gathers Benaiah's deeds — the lion, the two Moabite champions, and the giant Egyptian — under the single honour of 'a name among three mighty men.'
Ellicott: 'guard' means rather private audience — David set Benaiah over, or in, his council; Gill: more honourable than the thirty, below the first three.
Clarke: the Vulgate makes him privy counsellor ('put him to his ears'); some think a spy, recalling the Midas fable.

debates.question: What is 'David set him over his guard'?

sides.position: over the king's bodyguard
over, or in, the king's private council / confidence

schools.summary: Asahel, killed early by Abner, shows the list spans David's whole reign; leaving him aside the names are exactly thirty; this Elhanan differs from that of 2 Samuel 21:19.
Henry: Asahel, though slain early, keeps his place — honour once earned is not cancelled by death; Christ too has his worthies, their names written in heaven.
Gill: Asahel 'one of the thirty' or 'over the thirty'; Joab, the general, is named in no class.
Ellicott and Keil identify the men (Shammah perhaps Shamhuth, captain of the fifth division; Elika omitted in Chronicles) and note the frequent R/D variations.
Wesley lays down four rules for reconciling the lists: dialect, double names, more worthies than named, and replacements for the fallen.
Gill: Harodite for Harorite, by the frequent interchange of R and D.
Helez of Beth-pelet (general of the seventh division, called a Pelonite) and Ira of Tekoah (sixth month) placed in the monthly army courses.
Gill: Helez of 'Pater' (the Targum), elsewhere called the Pelonite.
Abiezer of Anathoth (Jeremiah's town, general for the ninth month); 'Mebunnai' a misspelling for Sibbechai the Hushathite, slayer of the giant Saph.
Gill: Abiezer of Anathoth in Benjamin, the birthplace of Jeremiah.
Zalmon (Chronicles Ilai — undecidable which is right) of the Ahohite family; Maharai of Netophah near Bethlehem, of the family of Serah, tenth division.
Gill: Zalmon the same with Ilai, a descendant of Ahoah, grandson of Benjamin.
Heleb (Heled/Heldai, general for the twelfth month) of Netophah; Ittai of Gibeah of Benjamin, to be distinguished from Ittai the Gittite of the flight from Absalom.
Gill: Heleb is called Heled in the parallel record.
Benaiah the Pirathonite (distinct from the son of Jehoiada; eleventh month) of Ephraim's Pirathon, and Hiddai of the brooks of Gaash — near the graves of Abdon and Joshua.
Gill: Benaiah of Pirathon, a city in the tribe of Ephraim.
Abi-albon (probably Abiel, the syllable strayed from Shaalbonite) of Beth-arabah in the Judean desert; Azmaveth 'the Baharumite,' of Bahurim of Shimei's curse.
Gill: Abialbon of Beth-arabah, called Abiel in the parallel.

themes: heroes from David's places of humiliation

schools.summary: Eliahba of Shaalbon in Dan; the middle phrase so corrupt that 'Bne-Jashen Jehonathan' yields no sense — Chronicles' 'Bne-hashem the Gizonite, Jonathan' is judged sounder.
Gill: Eliahba of Shaalbon or Shaalbin, a city in Dan, perhaps Josephus's Silbonitis.
Ellicott: read 'Jonathan the son of Shammah the Hararite' — so Jonathan, one of the thirty, was son of one of the first three; Keil agrees Chronicles is sounder.
Gill: Hararite as 'from the mountainous country,' the versions differing even to 'the mount of Olives.'
Eliam 'the son of Ahithophel the Gilonite' — a son of David's treacherous counsellor among his heroes; Chronicles differs so far that different persons may be meant.
Gill: Eliphelet is called Eliphal the son of Ur in the parallel record.

outliers.note: Notes it 'interesting' that a son of David's astute but treacherous counsellor Ahithophel stood among the thirty heroes.

themes: grace and treachery in one family

schools.summary: Hezro of Carmel in the mountains of Judah (of Nabal and Abigail's town, famous in David's early history); Paarai/Naarai the Arbite of Arab, form undecidable.
Gill: Hezrai of Mount Carmel, called Hezro in the parallel.
Igal (Chronicles Joel) of Zobah, connected with David's Syrian war; Bani the Gadite (Chronicles's 'Mibhar son of Hagri' corrupt) — the text here judged the true one.
Gill: Igal of Zobah, a kingdom in Syria, by Hillerus the same with Joel.

themes: the roll reaches beyond the border

schools.summary: Ellicott: Zelek the Ammonite one of several foreigners (Syrian, Gittite, Hittite) risen in David's service, probably proselytes; Naharai of Beeroth, chief of Joab's armour-bearers.
Gill: Zelek an Ammonite by birth and a proselyte, or so called from sojourn or exploits.
Ira and Gareb both Jithrites of Kirjath-jearim (the ark's resting-place); this Ira distinguished from the priest Ira of 2 Samuel 20:26.
Gill: the two of Jether, of the tribe of Judah by way of Caleb.
Keil vindicates the total 'thirty and seven'; Ellicott notes only thirty-six names appear, and that Chronicles adds sixteen more of later date.
Henry: to find Uriah the Hittite closing the roll revives and aggravates the memory of David's sin against a man who served him so well.
Gill: Uriah the Hittite, the husband of Bathsheba.
Clarke turns to textual matters — the sixteen further names in Chronicles and De Dieu's rendering of the oracle in verses 4-5.
